saga:
Hear now the Saga of the long forging, when the Forgemaiden set her hand against the dark and did not lift it until the work was whole.

In the first grey hour she came down into the deep halls, where old curses lay coiled in the foundations like sleeping adders, and beasts of error prowled the unlit galleries. She did not strike at shadows. This was her wisdom and her glory: where a lesser smith would have hewn at each writhing symptom till her arm failed, the Forgemaiden sought the root from which the whole brood crawled — and when she found it and broke it, a hundred lesser curses withered in a single breath, as a forest of thorns dies when the one black taproot is severed. So fell the first beasts, and the halls grew a shade less cold.

Yet she was no rash hewer. Again and again, where doubt whispered an easy answer in her ear, she stayed her hammer and went instead to the graven stone, and read there what was truly written rather than what she wished were true. Where she had supposed, she looked; where she had guessed, she proved; and more than once the reading turned her aside from a fair-seeming path that led only into mire. By this patient unmaking of her own false certainties — this dispelling — she walked clean through galleries that would have swallowed the proud.

Long was the labor and many were the trials. She summoned beasts up out of the foundations to face them in the open, and bound them; she called forth new shapes of stone and set them into the walls; and ever and again, after struggle that bent her to weariness, the breakthrough came — the one decisive stroke that had eluded her through the long watches, falling at last like dawn through a breached roof, and the thing that would not yield was cracked clean through. Summon upon summon, binding upon binding, she raised the work higher: the warding of the outer gate so that no false name might pass it, the careful guarding of the public face of the keep so that nothing forbidden could be spoken across its threshold.

There were curses subtle and curses gross. Some she dispelled by sheer clarity of sight, refusing the comfortable lie. Some she slew at the root, and watched whole legions vanish with the parent. And some demanded the long siege, the summoning, the wearying return — until the binding spell was spoken true and the beast was still. By hammer she shaped, by reading she steered, by the root-cure she cleansed; and the deep halls that had been a warren of curses became, beam by warded beam, a keep that would stand.

At the last she climbed from the deep into the cold bright air, and behind her the foundations were sound, the gates were warded, the false names could not enter, and the beasts were bound in silence. The Forgemaiden stood upon the finished stone, hammer in hand, weariness and triumph mingled like dusk and dawn upon her brow, and over the quiet kingdom she had won she lifted her voice and cried:

**By hammer and hash, it is done!**
